title: Pakistani Doodh Patti Chai

subs.name: 200 ml evaporated milk + 200 ml water
subs.notes: gives a richer, more condensed flavour

ingredients.name: loose black tea leaves (Lipton Yellow Label or Tapal Danedar)

subs.name: 2 tsp masala chai blend
subs.notes: adds ginger, clove, and pepper notes

ingredients.name: sugar
visual_checks: adjust to taste — doodh patti is traditionally quite sweet
ingredients.notes: adjust to taste — doodh patti is traditionally quite sweet

subs.name: 2 tsp jaggery (gur)
subs.notes: earthy sweetness that complements the tea

steps.text: Combine the milk, water, and cracked cardamom in a small saucepan. Bring to a vigorous simmer over medium heat, stirring occasionally. Do not let the milk boil unattended — it will rise rapidly.

Once simmering, add the loose tea leaves directly to the milk. Stir once to submerge. Reduce heat to low and simmer for 5 minutes, watching carefully and stirring every minute. The tea will gradually stain the milk a deep orange-brown.

Add the sugar and stir to dissolve. For a thicker, more intense chai, continue simmering for 2 more minutes — the liquid will reduce slightly and the tea flavour will deepen. For lighter chai, remove earlier.

Pour through a fine strainer directly into two cups or glasses. The chai should be a rich, creamy brown — not pale. Serve immediately while it is very hot, with a rusk, biscuit, or paratha on the side.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: milk → 200 ml evaporated milk + 200 ml water (gives a richer, more condensed flavour); sugar → 2 tsp jaggery (gur) (earthy sweetness that complements the tea); tea → 2 tsp masala chai blend (adds ginger, clove, and pepper notes)
indian_kitchen_adaptation: This is made exactly like south Indian filter coffee's milk-forward approach but with tea. Use any good full-cream milk — Amul or Heritage brands work well. Tapal Danedar tea is available in Pakistani grocery stores across Indian cities; alternatively use Brooke Bond Red Label or any strong Assam tea for the closest flavour. Simmer on the very lowest flame your hob allows to prevent the milk from scorching on the bottom.
